what_it_does: Defines a React-based equirectangular 360 degree panorama viewer that lazy-loads Three.js, renders an inside-out textured sphere, supports drag rotation, wheel zoom, projected hotspot overlays, optional hotspot creation by double-click, and exposes the component on window.Pano360.
observations: Loads Three.js from unpkg only when needed and caches the loading promise in module scope.
Uses React globals directly through destructured aliases rather than imports.
Builds and tears down a Three.js scene inside a useEffect tied to src, height, and serialized hotspots.
Projects hotspot longitude and latitude into screen coordinates and updates overlay DOM nodes directly inside the render loop.
Supports mouse, touch, wheel, resize, and double-click handlers on the renderer canvas.
The rendered help text advertises drag, wheel, and editable double-click behavior.
The fallback path after a Three.js load failure leaves the loading overlay visible rather than rendering a static image preview.
standouts: Combines WebGL rendering with HTML hotspot overlays pinned by projection math.
Uses direct DOM mutation for hotspot positions instead of React state updates on every animation frame.
Provides an editable mode for double-click hotspot creation.
conventions: Uses file-local helper functions nested inside the effect for Three.js scene behavior.
Stores imperative renderer state and cleanup on a React ref.
Uses inline styles for the viewer host, mount, overlay, and hotspot positioning.
Attaches the component to window instead of exporting it through an ES module.
Uses JSON.stringify(hotspots) in the effect dependency list to react to hotspot data changes.
concerns: onMoveHotspot is accepted as a prop but is not used.
hovering state is declared and set only in the unused animate function; the state value is not read.
animate is defined but never called because tick is the active animation loop.
Touch event listeners are added but not removed in cleanup.
project closes over the initial width and height, so hotspot overlay coordinates may become stale after resize.
Renderer disposal does not explicitly dispose the sphere geometry, material, or loaded texture.
The catch comment says a static image preview is used, but the JSX only shows the loading overlay when ready is false.
notes: This file is an imperative Three.js panorama viewer wrapped in a React component, with projected DOM hotspots layered over the canvas.
questions: Should onMoveHotspot support dragging existing hotspots or was it reserved for future behavior?
Should the failed-engine path render the source image as a static preview?
Should hotspot projection use current mount dimensions after resize?
Should touch listeners and Three.js resources receive fuller cleanup?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose mostly matches the implementation: it renders a 360 viewer with drag, wheel zoom, and pinned hotspot overlays; the stated static-image fallback is not implemented in the rendered output.
intention_signals: The file seems intended to provide a self-contained panorama inspection surface that can be dropped into a React page without bundling Three.js upfront, while allowing hotspots to be displayed or authored in place.
novelty: The distinct element is the mix of an inside-out Three.js sphere with HTML hotspots that are repositioned directly each animation frame from longitude and latitude coordinates.
contribution: Contributes a self-contained panoramic viewing substrate with on-demand Three.js loading, camera orientation controls, zoom, resize handling, and spatial hotspot overlay behavior that a fused system could keep as a 360 media interaction layer.
summary_for_embedding: React component implementing a lazy-loaded Three.js equirectangular panorama viewer with drag look controls, wheel zoom, projected HTML hotspots, editable double-click hotspot creation, and resize-aware canvas setup.
intention_for_embedding: This was for presenting and annotating 360 degree image spaces inside a design system without requiring Three.js to be loaded before the component mounts.

symbols.does: Loads Three.js from window or injects a script tag for the unpkg Three.js build.
symbols.description: This function centralizes runtime loading for Three.js and caches the promise so multiple calls share the same script request. It resolves with window.THREE when already present or after the script loads, and rejects on script load failure.

symbols.does: Renders the panorama host, initializes the Three.js viewer, and overlays optional hotspots and controls help text.
symbols.description: The component receives an image source, hotspot data, optional editing callbacks, height, and editable state. It creates the WebGL scene in an effect, positions hotspot DOM nodes over the canvas, supports interaction handlers, and displays a loading overlay until the Three.js setup calls setReady(true).

symbols.does: Updates the camera orientation from the current longitude and latitude values.
symbols.description: This nested function converts lon and lat degrees into spherical coordinates and calls camera.lookAt with a point on an implied sphere. It is invoked every animation frame before rendering.

symbols.does: Projects a hotspot longitude and latitude into canvas-relative x and y coordinates.
symbols.description: This function converts a hotspot's spherical coordinates into a Three.js vector, projects it through the camera, and maps normalized device coordinates into viewport pixels. It also returns an inFront flag based on the projected z value so overlay elements can be hidden when not visible.

symbols.does: Starts a drag interaction and records the pointer origin and starting camera angles.
symbols.description: This handler supports mouse and touch start events by reading client coordinates, setting dragging state, saving starting lon and lat, and changing the canvas cursor to grabbing.

symbols.does: Updates longitude and latitude while a drag interaction is active.
symbols.description: This handler ignores movement unless dragging is true, then computes pointer deltas from the original down event. Horizontal movement adjusts lon, and vertical movement adjusts lat with clamping between -85 and 85 degrees.

symbols.does: Ends a drag interaction and restores the grab cursor.
symbols.description: This handler clears the dragging flag for mouseup or touchend and resets the renderer canvas cursor. The event argument is accepted but not used.

symbols.does: Prevents page scrolling and changes the camera field of view for zooming.
symbols.description: This handler adjusts fov from the wheel delta, clamps it between 30 and 100, assigns it to the camera, and updates the projection matrix. It is registered with passive false so preventDefault can be honored.

symbols.does: Adds a hotspot at the double-clicked panorama point when editable mode is enabled.
symbols.description: This handler exits unless editable and onAddHotspot are present. It raycasts from the click position into the sphere, converts the hit point back into lon and lat, and passes those coordinates to onAddHotspot.

symbols.does: Defines an animation loop that updates camera look direction, renders the scene, and nudges React state.
symbols.description: This function calls setLook, renders the scene, invokes setHovering with an identity updater, and schedules itself with requestAnimationFrame. It is present in the file but not used because tick is the function that is called.

symbols.does: Repositions hotspot overlay elements to match their projected panorama coordinates.
symbols.description: This function finds DOM children marked with data-hot, maps each one to the corresponding hotspot by index, projects its lon and lat, and updates display and transform styles. It hides elements whose projected position is not in front.

symbols.does: Runs the active render loop for camera orientation, scene rendering, and hotspot overlay updates.
symbols.description: This function calls setLook, renders the Three.js scene, updates overlay DOM positions, and schedules the next frame through requestAnimationFrame. It is invoked once during setup and continues until cleanup cancels the frame.

symbols.does: Resizes the renderer and updates the camera aspect ratio when the mount changes size.
symbols.description: This handler reads the current mount width, applies it to the renderer with the fixed height prop, updates camera.aspect, and refreshes the projection matrix. It is connected to a ResizeObserver observing the mount element.
